subject: Cardiology
question: A 55-year-old African American man is diagnosed with stage 1 hypertension (BP 145/92 on 3 readings). He has no comorbidities and normal renal function. What is the most appropriate first-line antihypertensive agent?

options.explanation: ACE inhibitors are first-line in non-African American patients or those with CKD/diabetes. In African Americans, they are less effective as monotherapy due to lower renin levels. Either a CCB or thiazide is preferred first-line.

CCBs are first-line for African American patients with hypertension. They are equally effective regardless of renin status. Amlodipine is well-tolerated, once-daily dosing. Thiazide diuretics are also first-line in this population.

Beta-blockers are NOT first-line for uncomplicated hypertension. They are reserved for patients with compelling indications: CAD, heart failure, arrhythmias. They are less effective for stroke prevention compared to other agents.
Like ACE inhibitors, ARBs are less effective as monotherapy in African American patients due to lower renin levels. CCB or thiazide preferred first-line. ARBs are used when ACEi intolerant (cough).

Hydralazine is a third or fourth-line agent, not first-line. Used in resistant hypertension or in combination with nitrates in HFrEF in African Americans (A-HeFT trial). Causes reflex tachycardia and fluid retention.

educationalObjective: Select first-line antihypertensive based on patient demographics: CCB or thiazide for African Americans, ACEi/ARB or CCB for non-African Americans.
keyTakeaway: In African Americans with HTN: CCB or thiazide first-line. ACEi/ARB less effective as monotherapy. Add ACEi/ARB if proteinuric CKD.
